title: Template Literals Pro ✨
description: Master injecting code directly into your console outputs.

slides.title: The Concatenation Nightmare 🧟‍♂️
slides.content:
# The Old Way

We've briefly touched on Template Literals, but it's time to master them.

Combining strings and variables (concatenation) using the `+` sign is terrible. Trying to manage spaces and quotes causes endless bugs.

```javascript
// Pure misery
let text = "The user " + firstName + " " + lastName + " is " + age + " years old.";
```

Look at all those plus signs and empty quote spaces. It's unreadable.

slides.title: Backticks to the Rescue `
slides.content:
## The Modern Way

ES6 introduced **Template Literals**. Instead of using Single or Double quotes, you wrap the entire string in **Backticks** ` `. 

*(The backtick key is located right above the Tab key on most keyboards!)*

```javascript
// So much cleaner!
let text = `The user ${firstName} ${lastName} is ${age} years old.`;
```

When using backticks, you can magically inject variables directly into the text by wrapping them in **`${ }`**.

slides.title: Injecting Expressions 🧮
Try It Out 💻
slides.content: See how much easier it is to read and write Template Literals compared to old + concatenation.

quizData.question: Which of these is a perfectly valid Template Literal string?
quizData.explanation: If you try to use ${} inside single or double quotes, JavaScript will flat-out ignore it and just print out the literal symbols '${}'. You MUST use the Backtick `.

slides.title: Multi-Line Power 📜
slides.content:
## Another Superpower

Traditional strings cannot cross multiple lines in your code. They immediately break and cause a red syntax error.

Template Literals using Backticks allow for native multi-line strings! They literally respect your Enter/Return key spacing!

```javascript
let rawEmailText = `Hello Team,

We hit our goals this quarter!

Thanks,
Management`;
```

Notice we didn't have to use messy `\n` break symbols!
